data.title: Solana and XRP Prices Take a Tumble: What's Behind the Crypto Crash?
data.description:
Market turbulence hits Solana and XRP as scandals and regulatory uncertainties shake investor confidence.

Solana's Descent: Political Scandals and Memecoin Mayhem

Solana (SOL) has had a rough start to 2025, plunging more than 40% in just a month. The primary driver behind this decline? A perfect storm of political scandal, market sell-offs, and the lingering volatility of memecoins.

Reuters
@Reuters

In a new development, an Argentine judge was assigned to investigate President Javier Milei's role in promoting a cryptocurrency that crashed https://t.co/z9C0uJbzlB pic.twitter.com/k1ofHjKF1w

Feb 18, 2025

The trouble started when Argentine President Javier Milei openly endorsed LIBRA, a Solana-based memecoin that quickly became a cautionary tale in the crypto world. What initially seemed like a strong show of support from a sitting world leader turned into a nightmare when LIBRA’s market cap plummeted from $4.5 billion, losing 90% of its value. The project turned out to be a textbook pump-and-dump, leaving many retail investors empty-handed.

This spectacular collapse triggered a wave of sell-offs across the Solana network. Solana had already been riding high after a strong performance in late 2024, but the memecoin fiasco shattered investor confidence. Adding fuel to the fire, Milei himself is now facing legal action, with multiple lawsuits filed by Argentine lawyers alleging financial misconduct related to the LIBRA scandal.

Despite the chaos, some analysts remain optimistic, arguing that Solana’s fundamentals remain strong. VanEck, Bitwise, and Pantera Capital have all suggested that that SOL could still rally, with VanEck claiming it could reach to $520 by the end of 2025 if the current turbulence subsides and institutional interest remains strong.

XRP's Slump: Regulatory Rollercoaster and Political Letdowns

If Solana’s downfall was fueled by scandal, XRP’s woes stem from regulatory uncertainty and political letdowns.

X Screenshot, 9.40am, GMT+2, 19/02/25

XRP, which had a promising start to 2024, has dropped 3.6% to $2.59, marking a 19% decline over the past month. Much of this decline has been attributed to a mix of SEC-related concerns and fading optimism around crypto-friendly policies from the new Trump administration.

CryptoBull
@CryptoBull2020

Trump just posted about #XRP. I checked it out. It’s still there 🚀 pic.twitter.com/XTlg8MQJQM

Feb 19, 2025

One of the biggest hopes for XRP was the approval of an XRP Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF), which would have allowed institutional investors to gain exposure to XRP without directly buying the token. While analysts believe an approval could lead to huge inflows, the SEC has so far delayed any clear decision.

Adding to XRP’s troubles is the Trump administration’s unpredictable stance on crypto regulation. While candidate Trump hinted at potential pro-crypto policies—at one point even teasing a Bitcoin strategic reserve—his government’s actions so far have been lackluster. In fact, Trump’s new round of tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China has triggered broader market fears, leading to a general risk-off sentiment in crypto.

The ETF Tease: Hope on the Horizon?

Despite the bearish sentiment, some crypto bulls argue that XRP and Solana still have a chance to recover, thanks to potential ETF approvals.

Amonyx
@amonbuy

💥JUST IN: 🇺🇸 SEC acknowledges filing for Bitwise spot $XRP ETF. pic.twitter.com/KVAygLPYrS

Feb 18, 2025

Regarding XRP, the SEC is reviewing multiple ETF applications from Grayscale, Bitwise and more, and if approved, institutional investors could potentially inject vast sums. High levels of liquidity could significantly reverse the current downward trajectory.

Solana, too, has ETF potential, with investors showing interest in bringing a regulated Solana fund to the market. If approved, an ETF could help restore confidence in the ecosystem and push Solana higher. However, given recent controversies, regulators may be hesitant to greenlight anything related to Solana in the near term.

Proceed with Caution

While some long-term investors remain hopeful, the short-term outlook for Solana and XRP remains volatile.

Solana’s price may continue to feel the aftershocks of the LIBRA scandal, especially if the lawsuits against Milei gain traction. If more memecoins on Solana collapse, investors may start questioning the network’s ability to support long-term, serious projects.

XRP, on the other hand, is still caught in the regulatory crossfire. The SEC’s stance on crypto remains unpredictable, and until XRP gets clear regulatory approval—particularly in the form of an ETF—its price may remain stagnant.

For now, crypto investors should brace for more turbulence. Between political scandals, regulatory hurdles, and global market jitters, the coming months could be make-or-break for Solana and XRP.

One thing is clear: crypto is never boring.

data.title: Bayer Receives FDA Endorsement to Accelerate Development of Parkinson Treatment
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

Bayer's subsidiary AskBio received approval from the U.S. drug regulator to accelerate the development of its investigational gene therapy to treat moderate-stage Parkinson's disease.

The German pharmaceutical company said Wednesday that its treatment received advanced therapy designation from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration after its early-stage study showed slowed disease progression and improved motor outcomes in patients after 36 months.

The study demonstrated a favorable safety profile and the experimental drug was well tolerated by patients, the company said.

Bayer is currently recruiting patients for its mid-stage study of the experimental drug in the U.S.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: BHP Group Unit Prices US Offering of $3 Billion Bonds
data.description:
BHP Group (, BHG.JO) said Wednesday that its BHP Billiton Finance (USA) subsidiary priced a $3 billion senior unsecured bonds offering in the US.

The offer comprises $1 billion of 5% fixed-rate bonds due in 2030, $750 million of 5.125% fixed-rate bonds due in 2032, and $1.25 billion of 5.30% fixed-rate bonds due in 2035.

The mining company intends to use proceeds from the issuance for general corporate expenditures. Settlement is expected by Friday, subject to customary closing conditions.

data.title: Gansu Jiu Steel to Absorb Metal Products Trading Subsidiary
data.description:
Gansu Jiu Steel Group Hongxing Iron & Steel plans to absorb and merge with metal and mineral products trading subsidiary Urumqi Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

The Chinese iron and steel manufacturer will take over all of Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics' assets and liabilities.

Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics will be canceled after the completion of the merger.

data.title: Enersense to Boost Capacity at Boliden's Zinc Plant in Norway
data.description:
Enersense International was selected to expand Swedish metals producer Boliden's zinc facility in Odda, Norway.

The expansion aims to double the production at the plant, with production expected to begin during 2025, the Finnish energy company said Wednesday.

data.title: Zhejiang China Commodities City Names Vice Chairman
data.description:
Zhejiang China Commodities City Group's board of directors elected Bao Hua, a director, as vice chairman, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

Bao will also serve as a member of the board's Strategy and ESG Committee, the Chinese real estate developer said.

The company's shares closed 2% higher.

data.title: Zytronic Winds Down Assets After Failing to Sell Business
data.description:
By Michael Susin

Zytronic said it will start the wind-down of business assets after being unable to sell its trading subsidiary Zytronic Displays Ltd.

The U.K. listed company on Wednesday said so far there is no guarantee for the level of returns that may be available to shareholders.

Regarding its subsidiary, Zytronic said it was unable to agree on attractive terms despite engaging with multiple counterparties.

The outcome statement, which will provide shareholders with an indicative range of returns available, is being finalized and further announcements will be made in coming days.

On 14 Nov. the company said it would sell or wind down the business as part of a solvent liquidation of the company. The decision has been "reinforced by continuing weakness trading conditions with no material uplift in order intake."

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Aurobindo Pharma's Step-Down Unit Gets Five US FDA Observations
data.description:
Aurobindo Pharma step-down subsidiary Eugia Steriles received five observations from the US Food and Drug Administration following the inspection of its facility.

The regulator inspected the facility at Parawada Mandal in the Anakapalli District of Andhra Pradesh, Indian, from Feb. 10 to Feb. 18.

The pharmaceutical company said the observations were procedural and would be responded to within the stipulated time, according to a Tuesday filing to the stock exchanges.

data.title: Innovators Facade Systems' CEO Resigns
data.description:
Innovators Facade Systems Ltd said its chief executive officer, Raman Shivchand Sharma, has resigned from the position, effective March 1, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Sharma will continue to act as the whole-time director of the company.

Shares of the company were up nearly 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Singapore's Projected Fiscal Surplus for 2025 to Buffer Against Global Risks — Market Talk
data.description: The Singapore government's projection of a surplus in fiscal 2025 is likely to cushion against global uncertainties, according to CGS International economists in a note. They suggest that the government may have factored in the possibility that GDP growth and revenue outlooks could become uncertain if global conditions worsen. "This could prompt the authorities to embark on more expansionary policies to mitigate their impact while still keeping the fiscal position healthy," they add. CGS International maintains its forecast for Singapore's 2025 GDP growth at 2.5%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Gujarat Toolroom's Profit Plunges in Fiscal Q3; Shares Up 10%
data.description:
Gujarat Toolroom's consolidated profit nosedived to 14.3 million Indian rupees in the fiscal third quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024, from 143.2 million rupees a year ago.

Earnings per share contracted to 0.08 rupees compared with the year-ago figure of 2.58 rupees, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges gemstones and precious metals trader.

Revenue from operations in fiscal Q3, however, increased to 2.31 billion rupees from 1.31 billion rupees a year earlier.

Shares of the company were up nearly 10% in recent trade.

data.title: Able Global CEO Released from Remand, Resumes Duties
data.description:
Able Global's chief executive officer Edward Goh Swee Wang has been released from remand, according to a Tuesday Malaysian bourse filing.

He was released following his assistance in the ongoing investigation by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission regarding matters related to the private company affairs of its chairman Ng Keng Hoe.

The company noted that the CEO was remanded for probe, but has resumed duties, and business operations remain unaffected.

data.title: Indonesia Leaves Rate Steady as Expected
data.description:
The Bank of Indonesia decided to keep its benchmark interest rate at 5.75% during its February 2025 meeting, in line with market expectations.

The overnight deposit rate and lending facility rate were also kept unchanged at 5% and 6.5%, respectively.

data.title: Correct: Glencore Declares Dividend of 10c
Pret Composites to Construct Malaysian Factory for 750 Million Yuan
data.description:
Shanghai Pret Composites plans to invest 750 million yuan to construct a 2.5-gigawatt-hour cylindrical factory in Malaysia

The factory will manufacture batteries and accumulators, the Chinese prime and recycled materials compounder said.

The company's shares closed over 3% higher.

data.title: Trump Tariff Plans Could Weigh on Germany's DAX Index — Market Talk
data.description: Germany's DAX index could come under pressure today after President Trump spoke about new tariffs on cars, pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, IG says. "With new statements from Trump about tariffs, the DAX could possibly be the big loser of the day, as cars and pharmaceuticals are a large part of the index." However, it's unclear when these tariffs would be implemented and more information will come on April 2, IG says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Straumann Net Profit Rises as Higher Revenue Offsets FX Headwinds
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

Straumann Holding reported a rise in net profit for 2024, when higher revenue offset a hit by currency headwinds.

The Swiss dental-equipment manufacturer said on Wednesday that its full-year net profit rose to 338.3 million Swiss francs ($374.5 million) from 246.1 million francs the year before.

Core operating profit edged higher to 650.3 million francs from 636.7 million francs, despite a contraction in its margin to 26.0% from 28.0%.

Revenue rose 11% at constant currencies to 2.50 billion francs. In its fourth quarter, revenue was also up 11% at constant currency at 645.2 million francs. Higher sales in the Europe, Africa and Middle East Region offset softer consumer demand in North America which stabilized in the last quarter.

The company aims to propose a dividend increase to 0.95 francs from 0.85 francs the prior year, it said.

For 2025, the company said it remains confident in gaining market share. It aims to achieve organic revenue growth in the high single-digit percentage range, with a 30 to 60 basis-points improvement in its core earnings before interest and taxes margin at constant 2024 currency rates.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: BAE Systems Posts Double-Digit Jump in Sales as Defense Budgets Climb
data.description:
(07:00 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Sales GBP28.335B
(07:01 GMT) *BAE Systems Saw 2024 Average Sales GBP28.11B
(07:02 GMT) *BAE Systems Saw 2024 Average Underlying EPS 66.8p
(07:02 GMT) *Analysts BAE Systems 2024 Operating Profit GBP2.88B
(07:02 GMT) *Analysts Saw BAE Systems 2024 Revenue GBP28.21B
(07:02 GMT) *Analysts Saw BAE Systems 2024 EPS 65.04p
(07:04 GMT) *BAE Systems 2024 Order Intake GBP33.7B
(07:04 GMT) *Analysts Saw BAE Systems 2024 Order Intake GBP30.96B
(07:04 GMT) *BAE Systems 2024 Order Backlog GBP77.8B
(07:04 GMT) *Analysts Saw BAE Systems 2024 Order Backlog GBP74.45B
(07:05 GMT) *BAE Systems 2024 Order Book GBP60.4B
(07:05 GMT) *BAE Systems Raises Dividend to 33p Vs 30p
(07:06 GMT) *Analysts Saw BAE Systems 2024 Net Profit GBP1.95B
(07:16 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY EPS 64.1p
(07:16 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Net Pft GBP1.96B
(07:16 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Rev GBP26.31B
(07:16 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Oper Pft GBP2.69B
(07:16 GMT) *BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Pretax Pft GBP2.33B
(07:37 GMT) BAE Systems Posts Double-Digit Jump in Sales as Defense Budgets Climb

By Cristina Gallardo

BAE Systems posted double-digit growth in sales in 2024, boosted by growing defense budgets in its main markets.

The London-listed aerospace and defense manufacturer said Wednesday that sales reached 28.335 billion pounds ($35.74 billion) last year, up 14% on a constant currency basis from 25.28 billion pounds in 2023.

BAE, which makes a range of war equipment and provides cybersecurity services, said its order backlog reached 77.8 billion pounds as of the end of 2024, beating a Visible Alpha estimate of 74.45 billion pounds. The company's order intake was worth 33.7 billion pounds.

Underlying earnings before interest and taxes--the group's preferred measure of profitability--reached 3.015 billion pounds in 2024 compared with 2.68 billion pounds in 2023.

Net income stood at 1.96 billion pounds last year, up from 1.85 billion pounds the previous year, above a Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 1.95 billion pounds.

"Based on the exceptional visibility of our record order backlog and sustainability of our value-compounding business model, we remain confident in the positive momentum of our business into the future," Chief Executive Charles Woodburn said.

For 2025, BAE Systems expects sales to range between 29.17 billion and 31.59 billion pounds. Underlying EBIT should be between 3.26 billion and 3.50 billion pounds, it said.

The board declared a dividend of 20.6 pence, taking the total dividend to 33 pence a share, up 10% on year.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: BAE Systems Posts Higher FY24 Attributable Profit, Revenue
data.description:
BAE Systems (BA.L) said Wednesday its profit and revenue for 2024 rose year over year.

Profit attributable to shareholders for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 1.96 billion pounds sterling, compared with 1.86 billion pounds earlier. EPS moved to 0.641 pound from 0.604 pound.

The aerospace and defense company's revenue was 26.31 billion pounds, compared with 23.08 billion pounds a year ago.

Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting 28.14 billion pounds in sales, 1.98 billion pounds in net income, and 0.67 pounds in EPS.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a final dividend of 0.206 pound per share, payable June 2 to shareholders on record April 22. It paid 0.185 pound per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to 0.33 pound per share, up from 0.30 pound per share in the previous year.

data.title: Glencore's Earnings Fall; Plans $2.2 Billion in Shareholder Returns
data.description:
(07:02 GMT) *Glencore Announces Around $2.2B of Shareholder Returns
(07:03 GMT) *Glencore Declares Dividend of 10c
(07:04 GMT) *Glencore Declares 'Top-Up' Buyback of $1.0B
(07:04 GMT) *Analysts Has Seen Glencore 2024 Revenue $232.93B
(07:05 GMT) *Glencore 2024 Adjusted EBITDA $14.36B
(07:05 GMT) *Analysts Had Seen Glencore 2024 Adjusted EBITDA $14.61B
(07:05 GMT) *Glencore Adjusted EBIT $6.94B
(07:06 GMT) *Glencore CEO: Decline in Adjusted EBITDA Due to Lower Average Energy Coal Prices YOY
(07:35 GMT) *Correct: Glencore Declares Dividend of 10c
(07:39 GMT) Glencore's Earnings Fall; Plans $2.2 Billion in Shareholder Returns

By Adam Whittaker

Glencore's earnings fell declined on lower coal prices, missing market views, but the mining and trading giant still plans to return around $2.2 billion to shareholders.

The commodities giant said Wednesday that adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization fell 16% to $14.36 billion. This compared with analysts' expectations of $14.61 billion, according to a consensus compiled by Visible Alpha.

Glencore said its results reflect the progressive normalization of energy markets after the severe disruptions seen over 2022-23 during the start of the Russian-Ukraine conflict.

Thermal coal and gas prices materially declined but the market for copper and zinc concentrates was favorable, it said.

Revenue rose 6% on year to $230.94 billion, compared with $232.93 billion analysts had expected.

The miner swung to a $1.63 billion net loss from $4.28 billion in profit a year earlier, as it booked $5.3 billion in impairments, including at its South African coal operations, the company said.

The miner announced around $2.2 billion in shareholder returns and said it will recommend a base dividend of $0.10 a share and launch a buyback of $1 billion.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Prime Focus Arm Brahma Buys Metaphysic
data.description:
Prime Focus subsidiary Brahma acquired Metaphysic, a generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) technology developer to create photorealistic content.

The post-transaction valuation of the combined entity, Brahma and Metaphysic, stands at $1.43 billion, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

Under the deal, Abu Dhabi-based investor United Al Saqer Group (UASG) and Brahma parent DNEG (Double Negative) will invest an additional $25 million into Brahma.

This follows UASG's $200 million strategic investment in the DNEG Group in 2024.

data.title: Hong Kong Technology Venture's Average Daily GMV Decreases 4.5% Year-on-Year in January
data.description:
Hong Kong Technology Venture's e-commerce business' average daily gross merchandise value on order intake decreased by 4.5% to HK$23.1 million in January from HK$24.2 million recorded in January 2024, while it increased 4.5% from HK$22.1 million logged in December 2024.

The monthly GMV on order intake for the reporting month was HK$717 million, up 4.7% from HK$685 million last month, according to a Tuesday filing with the Hong Kong Exchange.

The number of unique customers fell slightly to 593,000 in the reporting month from 594,000 in December 2024, and from 623,000 in January 2024.

The number of active users of the HKTVmall application reached nearly 1.6 million in January 2025, lower than almost 1.6 million in the prior month, the filing said.

data.title: Conduit Holdings Posts Lower FY24 Total Income; Reinsurance Revenue Up
data.description:
Conduit Holdings said Wednesday its total comprehensive income for 2024 declined, while revenue grew year over year.

Total comprehensive income for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was $125.6 million, compared with $190.8 million earlier. EPS moved to $0.79 from $1.19.

The London-listed reinsurer's reinsurance revenue was $813.7 million, compared with $633 million a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board declared a final dividend of $0.18 per share for the period, payable April 17 to shareholders on record March 21. It paid $0.18 per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to $0.36 per share, unchanged on an annual basis.

data.title: CLSA Premium's New Major Shareholder Will Offer to Buy Remaining Shares at HK$0.1229
data.description:
CLSA Premium said Beijing Tong Ren Tang (Cayman) bought 813,316,000 shares, representing 40% of its total issued shares, from CITIC Securities International for HK$98.0 million at HK$0.1205 apiece.

After the completion, the buyer and the vendor, as in concert parties, in aggregate hold 1,200,310,001 shares, representing 59.03% of the total issued share capital of the company.

Shanggu Securities, on behalf of the buyer, will make an offer to buy all the shares not owned by the buyer and in concert parties at HK$0.1229.

data.title: Shuaa Capital Offers $75 Million Bond Exchange for Mandatory Convertible Bonds
data.description:
Shuaa Capital's (DFM:SHUAA) Shuaa Capital Bond 1 offered to exchange up to $75 million of its outstanding $150 million 7.5% notes due March 31 for Emirati dirham-denominated mandatory convertible bonds.

The asset management and investment company said Wednesday that for each $1,000 principal amount of existing notes exchanged, bondholders will receive 3,672.50 dirhams in principal amount of mandatory convertible bonds, subject to the maximum acceptance amount.

The company's bondholders have until Feb. 28 to accept the exchange offer, subject to approval from the United Arab Emirates' Securities and Commodities Authority. The offer's settlement is scheduled for March 18.

data.title: What are the main events for today?
data.description:
The main events today have been the RBNZ policy decision and the UK CPI report. The RBNZ cut interest rates by 50 bps as expected but Governor Orr sounded less dovish than expected saying that the 3.75% was the high end of the range of neutral rates while previously he said it was 3.50%.

The UK Core CPI, on the other hand, came in line with expectations while the Services CPI Y/Y measure missed slightly coming in at 5.0% vs. 5.1% expected.

Looking ahead, we have the US housing data (Housing Starts and Building Permits) in the American session and then the FOMC Meeting Minutes late in the day. These shouldn't be market moving releases as the housing data is not in market's focus right now and the FOMC Minutes are three-weeks old data.

Central bank speakers:

  • 19:10 GMT/14:10 ET - RBNZ's Orr (neutral - voter)
  • 22:00 GMT/17:00 ET - Fed's Jefferson (neutral - voter)

data.title: Hongchang Electrical Technology to Offload NEV Parts Developing Subsidiary for 21 Million Yuan; Shares Up 5%
data.description:
Zhejiang Hongchang Electrical Technology agreed to sell 66% of new energy vehicle parts developing subsidiary Suzhou Nascondi Auto Parts for 20.5 million yuan, accorindg to a

The stake will be sold to individual investor Zhang Chunhua.

After the transaction is completed, the Chinese electrical components manufacturer will no longer hold Nascondi Auto Parts.

The company's shares jumped less than 5% in recent trade.

data.title: UK Market Talk Roundup: Brokers Comments
data.description:
Broker comments in the UK today. Compiled by Dow Jones Newswires Markets Desk, markets.eu@dowjones.com

0733 GMT - The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - HSBC's medium-term guidance for its return on tangible equity will likely be a focus in management's call with analysts and investors, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note after the bank's results. The assumptions underpinning the guidance will prompt questions, analysts Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say. HSBC kept its midterm ROTE guidance unchanged in the mid-teens excluding notable items, while consensus pencils in a 14% return. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0704 GMT - HSBC produced a respectable set of fourth-quarter numbers, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. The bank's adjusted pretax profit beat consensus on better income and costs, which were partially offset by higher impairments, analysts Elise Yu Ge and Edward Firth say. Its $2 billion share buyback feels light against its recent $3 billion run rate but the dividend it proposed was slightly better, they write. HSBC is the most expensive U.K. bank on a price-to-earnings basis, which is difficult to justify for a group delivering little loan growth and targeting mid-teens returns, the analysts say. "Some may argue that HSBC has traditionally traded at a premium to U.K. banks, but to suggest that Asia/Hong Kong is the same opportunity as it was 15 year ago is obviously flawed," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

Contact us in London. +44-20-7842-9288
Markettalk.eu@dowjones.com

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0733 GMT - The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Inflation rose faster than expected at the start of the year but the Bank of England is unlikely to be deterred from its plans for gradual rate-cuts this year, writes Joe Nellis, economic adviser to consultancy MHA. Consumer prices rose 3% on year in January, picking up more pace than economists had estimated, figures show Wednesday. But that acceleration was down to regulated prices--such as energy and value-added taxes--rather than underlying pressures, Nellis notes. "A close watch will be kept on price rises for some months ahead, but the Bank will be content knowing that sustainable inflationary pressures remain cooled," he tells clients. "At least for now."(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0719 GMT - Singapore Exchange will likely benefit in the short to medium term from government measures aiming to boost the local equities market, CGS International analysts say in a note. Tax incentives were introduced in the Singapore budget announced Tuesday. These include tax rebates and concessionary tax rates for companies looking to list on SGX, as well as tax incentives for fund managers that invest substantially in Singapore-listed equities, they write. These measures could raise net new money inflows, local market trading turnover, liquidity and overall regional interest in the Singapore market, they say. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - ASR Nederland's results should have a moderately positive impact on its shares, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's 2024 print. The group's organic capital generation for the second half was 5% higher than consensus expectations, analyst Michele Ballatore writes. "The 5pps beat on Solvency (198%) is also a positive development, with markets impact possibly looking lower than expected," he notes. He also flags the positive development in the non-life combined ratio and good momentum in the defined contribution space in the life segment. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Nordic markets may open little changed with IG calling the OMXS30 flat at around 2757. U.S. stock markets moved cautiously higher yesterday after Monday's holiday for Presidents Day. President Trump voiced new tariff threats yesterday and mentioned, among other things, that there will probably be tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. "This has led to a somewhat more mixed development in Asia." The U.S. and Russia met yesterday in Saudi Arabia for a first meeting on peace in Ukraine. Trump expressed optimism after the meeting and said that they have agreed on more meetings in the future, but the road to peace will probably not be easy, SEB adds. OMXS30 closed at 2757.28, OMXN40 at 2644.78 and OBX at 1409.98. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0712 GMT - Chinese shares closed higher, supported by semiconductor and technology stocks. Investor sentiment remains boosted after President Xi's meeting with business leaders, as markets await the Two Sessions meeting in early March for more policy signs. Among the top gainers, Will Semiconductor rose 10%, National Silicon Industry gained 15% and Shenzhen Inovance Technology was up 8.6%. Meanwhile, bank and pharmaceutical stocks led losses. Bank of Ningbo fell 2.0% and Sichuan Kelun Pharmaceutical closed 1.9% lower. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index closed 0.8% higher at 3351.76, the Shenzhen Composite Index gained 1.9% and the ChiNext Price Index rose 2.1%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0712 GMT - Markets this morning will be processing President Trump's plan to impose 25% tariffs on imported cars, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals, analysts at LBBW Research say. Trump announced the tariffs Tuesday, adding that they could rise substantially over the course of the year and might come on top of reciprocal tariffs the administration says will equalize U.S. tariffs with the duties charged by other countries. "This would hit the export-oriented German automotive industry hard," LBBW Research adds. Trump plans to announce further details at the beginning of April. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - HSBC's medium-term guidance for its return on tangible equity will likely be a focus in management's call with analysts and investors, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note after the bank's results. The assumptions underpinning the guidance will prompt questions, analysts Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say. HSBC kept its midterm ROTE guidance unchanged in the mid-teens excluding notable items, while consensus pencils in a 14% return. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0709 GMT - Europe's zippy stock market might point to hope that current travails will catalyse bolder action on the continent, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note. The Stoxx Europe 600 has hit new heights this year, driven by defense stocks amid plans for a step-up in military spending. That buoyancy might seem odd given Europe's fiscal, political and trade problems, Vistesen and Debono say. But those challenges might precisely be so great that investors see drastic action as the only option in Europe's capitals, they say. "[B]etting on Europe to get its act together means betting on stronger economic and political integration, and fiscal stimulus," the economists say. "Lots of fiscal stimulus." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0704 GMT - Singapore's equities market should react positively to the latest government budget with investors and businesses viewing it favorably, says Vasu Menon, OCBC's managing director of investment strategy. "However, don't expect major fireworks given the global uncertainties ahead especially with regards to Trump's policies and the risk of a trade war," he says in a note. Singapore is an open and small economy, which will be affected by global developments, especially those relating to China and the U.S. While the latest budget may help to some extent, it may not be sufficient fodder to fuel a sustainable rally, he adds.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0704 GMT - HSBC produced a respectable set of fourth-quarter numbers, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. The bank's adjusted pretax profit beat consensus on better income and costs, which were partially offset by higher impairments, analysts Elise Yu Ge and Edward Firth say. Its $2 billion share buyback feels light against its recent $3 billion run rate but the dividend it proposed was slightly better, they write. HSBC is the most expensive U.K. bank on a price-to-earnings basis, which is difficult to justify for a group delivering little loan growth and targeting mid-teens returns, the analysts say. "Some may argue that HSBC has traditionally traded at a premium to U.K. banks, but to suggest that Asia/Hong Kong is the same opportunity as it was 15 year ago is obviously flawed," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - The car industry is back in focus after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Analysts at Nomura noted that the U.S. is the largest export market outside of the eurozone for German car manufacturers, and Trump has taken particular issue with the European automotive sector and Europeans not buying U.S. cars. "Some car manufacturers already forewarned factory closures and layoffs prior to Trump's election in November 2024, and the prospect of U.S. tariffs only worsens the situation," they write. Nomura also expects the U.S. administration to apply reciprocal tariffs sometime in April. Currently, the U.S. has a 2.5% tariff on European cars, while the EU charges U.S. imports 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE 100 Seen Opening Flat After U.K. Inflation Data — Market Talk
data.description: The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)
